schools.summary: The writer (commonly identified as James the Just, the Lord's brother and head of the Jerusalem church) calls himself 'a servant (doulos — bondservant) of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ' — a title of humble devotion, not asserting his apostleship or kinship to Jesus but his servanthood, the coupling of God and the Lord Jesus marking Christ's dignity; 'to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad (in the dispersion, the diaspora)' — the Jewish people scattered among the nations, specifically those of Jewish origin who had embraced the Christian faith (Jewish believers of the dispersion); 'greeting (chairein — the common Greek epistolary greeting),' the same form James used in the Jerusalem council letter (Acts 15:23), a small mark of authenticity (Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Clarke, Ellicott).

themes: James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ
to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad, greeting
the writer, James, addressing the scattered twelve tribes
the dispersed Jewish Christians

schools.summary: The striking exhortation: 'my brethren, count it (reckon, regard it) all joy (pure, complete joy) when ye fall into (peripesete — encounter, are surrounded by) divers (poikilois — various, manifold) temptations' — believers regarding their trials, of every kind, as occasion for the fullest joy; 'temptations (peirasmois)' meaning here not solicitations to sin but trials, afflictions, testings (the various outward troubles and persecutions that prove faith, the word shifting to mean solicitation to evil later, v.13); the joy grounded not in the pain itself but in its fruit, the testing of faith working patience (v.3) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: the believer counts trial joy not for the suffering but for what it produces — the proving of faith and the working of patience; knowing the good that trials bring forth, the Christian can rejoice even in hardship, as the athlete welcomes the contest.

themes: My brethren, count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations
the call to count trials all joy
trials, not solicitations to sin
joy grounded in the fruit of testing

schools.summary: The reason trials are joy: 'knowing this, that the trying (dokimion — the testing, proving, as metal is assayed) of your faith worketh (produces, brings about) patience (hypomonen — steadfast endurance)' — the believer knowing that the testing of faith yields endurance, this knowledge being the ground of the joy commanded in v.2; 'the trying' the process by which faith is proved genuine (as gold is tested by fire, assaying faith, showing it real and purifying it), which 'worketh patience' — produces steadfast endurance, the habit of patiently bearing up under trial, the very fruit that makes trials valuable (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the trying of your faith worketh patience
the trying of faith works patience
faith assayed as gold by fire
endurance the fruit of testing

schools.summary: The exhortation: 'but let patience have her perfect work (let endurance complete its operation, carry its work to full maturity), that ye may be perfect (teleioi — mature, complete) and entire (holokleroi — whole, sound in every part), wanting (lacking) nothing' — patience to be allowed to finish its work so that the believer becomes fully mature and complete; the twofold aim of 'perfect (mature, fully developed) and entire (whole, lacking no part)' being complete Christian character, sound and entire, deficient in nothing — the full-grown, well-rounded believer, the perfecting work of patience leaving him lacking in no grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: let patience have her perfect work, that ye may be perfect and entire, wanting nothing
the call to let patience complete its work
perfect and entire, complete character
the fullness of Christian maturity

schools.summary: The remedy for deficiency: 'if any of you lack (is deficient in) wisdom (the practical wisdom to bear trials rightly, to understand God's dealings), let him ask of God, that giveth to all (who ask) liberally (haplos — simply, generously, freely, without grudging), and upbraideth not (does not reproach, scold for asking or for past faults); and it shall be given him' — the believer who lacks wisdom (especially to profit from trials) to ask it from God in prayer; God giving freely and without rebuke (unlike grudging human givers), and the promise sure that the wisdom asked will be granted, so the believer may pray with confidence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: God gives generously and without reproach to all who ask; the wisdom needed to bear trials is granted freely to the prayerful, and the promise 'it shall be given' assures the believer that his asking will not be in vain.

themes: If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not
the promise of wisdom to those who ask God
God giving freely and without reproach
prayed with confidence

schools.summary: The condition of the promise: 'but let him ask in faith (believing, trusting God's power and willingness to give), nothing wavering (diakrinomenos — not doubting, not divided, not hesitating). For he that wavereth is like a wave (klydon — a surge, billow) of the sea driven with the wind and tossed (blown about)' — the prayer for wisdom to be offered in faith without doubt, wavering (the divided, doubting mind that hesitates between believing and disbelieving) disqualifying the asker; true prayer requiring settled confidence in God, not a hesitating spirit; the doubter, like a restless wave, driven and tossed by every gust, unstable and never settled — the vivid picture of the doubting mind (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: let him ask in faith, nothing wavering
he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed
asking in faith, not wavering
the doubter unstable as a tossed wave

schools.summary: The consequence of doubt: 'for let not that man (the waverer of v.6) think (suppose, expect) that he shall receive any thing of the Lord' — the doubting, divided man not to expect to obtain anything from God, his unbelief forfeiting the answer; he receives nothing (not the wisdom asked, nor any blessing), doubt cutting off the supply, God answering the prayer of faith not of the divided heart; no arbitrary refusal but the nature of the case, for the doubter, not truly trusting God, is in no condition to receive (faith being the hand that takes God's gift, which the waverer lacks) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord
the wavering man receives nothing from the Lord
doubt forfeiting the answer
faith the hand that receives

schools.summary: The description of the waverer: 'a double minded man (dipsychos — two-souled, of two minds, divided in loyalty between God and the world or between faith and doubt) is unstable (akatastatos — restless, inconstant, wavering) in all his ways' — the man of divided mind unsteady in his whole conduct, not just in prayer; 'double minded' describing one whose heart is divided, wavering between trusting God and trusting self or the world, a soul not wholly given to God but torn between two allegiances; 'unstable in all his ways' — the divided heart producing an inconstant, unsettled character in everything he does, throughout his life (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: A double minded man is unstable in all his ways
the double-minded man unstable in all his ways
the divided heart torn between God and the world
pervasive instability

schools.summary: The exhortation to the poor believer: 'let the brother of low degree (the poor, humble, lowly in worldly station or rank) rejoice (boast, glory) in that he is exalted (in his high spiritual station, his exaltation in Christ)' — the lowly believer to rejoice in the spiritual dignity that is his in Christ, however poor his earthly lot; many early Christians being poor, James addressing their condition with comfort; the poor brother 'exalted' — raised to high dignity as a child of God and an heir of the kingdom, glorying not in wealth but in his exaltation in grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Let the brother of low degree rejoice in that he is exalted
the lowly brother rejoicing in his exaltation
the poor raised to dignity in Christ
glorying in grace, not wealth

schools.summary: The parallel exhortation: 'but the rich (the wealthy believer), in that he is made low (in his humiliation, the lowering of his earthly pride, or his learning to count earthly riches as nothing): because as the flower of the grass (the wild flower) he shall pass away (perish, fade)' — the rich believer to glory not in his wealth but in being humbled, in the abasement that teaches him the vanity of riches (whether his being brought low by trial or his Christian lowliness of mind that no longer trusts in riches, glorying in what the world counts loss); for the rich man and his riches are as fleeting as a flower, his wealth and even his life quickly fading (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But the rich, in that he is made low
because as the flower of the grass he shall pass away
the rich brother glorying in being made low
the vanity of fleeting riches

schools.summary: The vivid picture: 'for the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat (the scorching east wind, the kauson), but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth, and the grace (beauty, comeliness) of the fashion (appearance, form) of it perisheth: so also shall the rich man fade away (wither, perish) in his ways (in his pursuits, his goings, his very course of life)' — the rising sun with its burning heat quickly withering the grass, dropping its flower, and destroying all its beauty (the swift destruction of fragile loveliness); so the rich man, busy in his affairs, will wither like the flower, his wealth and life fading even as he pursues them — the warning against trusting in riches (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat, but it withereth the grass
so also shall the rich man fade away in his ways
the rich man fading like the scorched flower
the warning against trusting riches

schools.summary: The beatitude returning to the theme of trials: 'blessed (happy, to be congratulated) is the man that endureth (hypomenei — patiently bears up under) temptation (trial, peirasmon): for when he is tried (dokimos — approved, having stood the test), he shall receive the crown (stephanos — the victor's wreath) of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him' — the one who steadfastly endures testing being blessed, and having passed the trial receiving the crown which is life (eternal life), the reward of the proved and faithful; the crown promised to those who love God, love for God being the mark of the genuine believer who endures (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Blessed is the man that endureth temptation
he shall receive the crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him
the blessing and crown promised to the endurer of trial
love for God the mark of the endurer

schools.summary: The shift in the word 'tempted': 'let no man say when he is tempted (peirazomenos — now solicited to sin, enticement to evil, not mere trial), I am tempted of (from) God: for God cannot be tempted with (by) evil (apeirastos — incapable of being solicited by evil, untemptable), neither tempteth he (solicits to evil) any man' — no one, when drawn toward sin, may blame God as the author of the solicitation; God himself wholly removed from evil (neither susceptible to it nor having any part in it), so he cannot be the source of solicitation to sin; the key distinction (reconciling with God's 'tempting' Abraham, Genesis 22, and 'lead us not into temptation') being that God tests and tries men (trials, providential testing, are from him) but never entices anyone to sin — the trial that proves faith is from God, the solicitation to evil never his (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Let no man say when he is tempted, I am tempted of God
for God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man
God tempts no man to evil
trial from God, but not solicitation to sin

schools.summary: The real origin of solicitation to sin: 'but every man is tempted, when he is drawn away (exelkomenos — dragged off, lured away, as by a baited hook) of his own lust (epithymias — desire, craving), and enticed (deleazomenos — baited, allured, as a fish to bait)' — temptation arising from within, from a man's own corrupt desire, which lures and baits him toward sin; the source being 'his own lust' (not God, not merely the devil or the world, but the man's own appetite, the inward craving that draws him); the language drawn from hunting and fishing, lust acting like a hunter luring prey or a baited hook drawing a fish, the sinner caught by his own desire (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust, and enticed
temptation springs from a man's own lust
the inward craving the source of sin
lured as a fish to bait

schools.summary: The figure of conception and birth: 'then when lust (desire) hath conceived (sullabousa — become pregnant), it bringeth forth (gives birth to) sin: and sin, when it is finished (apotelestheisa — full-grown, completed, brought to maturity), bringeth forth death' — desire, when yielded to, conceiving and giving birth to actual sin (the inward craving, embraced, becoming the outward act); and sin, when it has run its full course and matured, producing death (spiritual and eternal death the offspring of completed sin); the deadly genealogy tracing the course from inward desire, through its conception into act, to its final issue in death, each step begetting the next, showing the fatal end of yielding to lust (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin
sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death
the deadly genealogy: lust conceives sin, sin brings forth death
the fatal course of yielding to desire

schools.summary: The transition: 'do not err (me planasthe — be not deceived, do not be led astray), my beloved brethren' — a tender warning against the error just refuted (that God is the author of temptation, vv.13-15) and an introduction to the truth that follows (that God is the giver of all good, v.17); the warning guarding against a fundamental error about God's character (supposing that evil comes from him), the affectionate address 'my beloved brethren' softening the correction with love, James earnest that they not be misled on this vital point (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Do not err, my beloved brethren
the warning not to be deceived
guarding against error about God's character
the affectionate hinge of the argument

schools.summary: The truth answering the error: 'every good gift (dosis — the act of giving, every good giving) and every perfect gift (dorema — the gift bestowed) is from above (from heaven), and cometh down from the Father of lights (the Creator and source of all lights — the heavenly luminaries, and so of all light, material and spiritual), with whom is no variableness (parallage — change, variation), neither shadow of turning (trope — no shadow cast by turning, no eclipse or alteration)' — all good of every kind coming down from above (God the author of good, not evil); God, who made and governs the lights of heaven, the Father and fountain of all illumination and good; and unlike the heavenly bodies that rise, set, and cast shifting shadows, God utterly unchanging in his goodness, never varying (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the 'Father of lights' is unchanging, unlike the created luminaries that wax, wane, and are eclipsed; with God there is no variation nor shadow of turning, his goodness constant and his gifts ever good — the source of every good and perfect gift, from whom no evil can come.

themes: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights
with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning
every good gift comes from the unchanging Father of lights
God constant in goodness, unlike the shifting heavenly lights

schools.summary: The chief instance of God's good gift: 'of his own will (bouletheis — having willed it, by his own free purpose and choice) begat he us (apekyesen — gave us birth, regenerated us) with (by means of) the word of truth (the gospel, the message of truth), that we should be a kind of firstfruits (aparche — the first and choicest portion, consecrated to God) of his creatures (his creation)' — God, of his own free will and sovereign good pleasure, giving us spiritual birth (the new birth the supreme example of his good giving), wrought through the instrumentality of the gospel; believers, regenerated, being as the firstfruits of God's new creation, the first and consecrated portion, a pledge of the larger harvest to come (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Of his own will begat he us with the word of truth
that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures
the new birth by God's will through the word of truth
believers the firstfruits of the new creation

schools.summary: The practical wisdom flowing from the word of truth (v.18): 'wherefore, my beloved brethren, let every man be swift to hear (quick, ready to listen — especially to the word of God and to instruction), slow to speak (not hasty, not rash with the tongue, not quick to talk, teach, or assert oneself — the curbing of the tongue, a major theme of the epistle), slow to wrath (slow to anger, not easily provoked, restraint of temper, especially in receiving the word or in dispute)' — the reason following (v.20) that human wrath does not work God's righteousness; the threefold counsel of eager listening, restrained speech, and slow anger (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath
the threefold counsel: swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath
eager listening, restrained speech, slow anger
practical wisdom from the word

schools.summary: The ground of v.19's counsel: 'for the wrath (anger, the angry temper) of man worketh not (does not produce, does not accomplish) the righteousness of God (the righteousness God requires and approves)' — human anger not bringing about the righteous conduct or character that God desires, passion not serving God's holy ends; 'the wrath of man' being the angry, passionate spirit native to fallen men (restless and destructive, not productive of holiness, hindering rather than advancing God's cause); it neither produces personal righteousness nor furthers God's righteous purposes but breeds strife and sin, hence the believer is to be slow to wrath (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For the wrath of man worketh not the righteousness of God
human wrath does not work God's righteousness
passion breeding strife, not holiness
the reason to be slow to anger

schools.summary: The negative and positive commands: 'wherefore lay apart (put off, strip away, as filthy garments) all filthiness (rhyparian — moral defilement, uncleanness) and superfluity of naughtiness (the overflow, excess, of malice or wickedness), and receive (welcome) with meekness (gentleness, a teachable, submissive spirit, not the wrath just condemned, v.20) the engrafted (emphyton — implanted, ingrafted) word, which is able to save your souls' — the believer casting off all moral filth and the abundance of wickedness (as one strips off dirty clothing), and meekly welcoming the implanted word of the gospel, which, received and obeyed, has saving power and brings the salvation of the soul (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: lay apart all filthiness and superfluity of naughtiness
receive with meekness the engrafted word, which is able to save your souls
the call to put away evil and receive the engrafted word
the implanted word with saving power

schools.summary: The heart of the matter: 'but be ye doers (poietai — practicers, those who carry out) of the word, and not hearers only (akroatai — mere listeners, as at a lecture), deceiving (paralogizomenoi — deluding by false reasoning) your own selves' — the believer not merely listening to the word but practicing it, obedience (not mere hearing) being required; to be a 'hearer only' is to listen to the word without obeying it (a fatal mistake, mere hearing profiting nothing, the word needing to be acted upon); and such a hearer deceives himself, imagining that hearing alone makes him right with God — a dangerous self-delusion, mistaking acquaintance with the truth for obedience to it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves
the call to be doers, not hearers only
obedience required, not mere hearing
the self-deception of empty hearing

schools.summary: The figure: 'for if any be a hearer of the word, and not a doer, he is like unto a man beholding (katanoounti — observing, looking at) his natural face (the face he was born with) in a glass (esoptron — a mirror)' — the hearer who does not obey like a man who looks at his own face in a mirror, seeing himself as he is (perhaps his flaws and need) but only momentarily; the word being like a mirror that reveals a man to himself, showing his spiritual face and faults, which the hearer-only glances in but does not act on (the comparison developed in v.24) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: if any be a hearer of the word, and not a doer, he is like unto a man beholding his natural face in a glass
the hearer-only likened to a man glancing in a mirror
the word a mirror revealing the self
seen but not acted on

schools.summary: The completion of the figure: 'for he beholdeth himself (looks at his reflection), and goeth his way (departs), and straightway (immediately, at once) forgetteth what manner of man he was' — the man glancing in the mirror, walking off, and instantly forgetting what he saw of himself (the fleeting, ineffective look); the impression made by the mirror momentary, lost as soon as he turns away, changing nothing; so the hearer-only, having heard the word and seen his condition, goes away and forgets — the word making no lasting impression, producing no change, leaving him none the better for having heard (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he beholdeth himself, and goeth his way, and straightway forgetteth what manner of man he was
the man forgetting at once what he saw
the fleeting look that changes nothing
the futility of mere hearing

schools.summary: The contrast with the glancing hearer: 'but whoso looketh into (parakypsas — stoops down to gaze intently, peers carefully) the perfect law of liberty (the gospel, the law that is perfect and that brings freedom from sin's bondage), and continueth therein (remains, abides in it — not a passing glance but an abiding attention, keeping looking and obeying), he being not a forgetful hearer (who forgets, v.24), but a doer of the work (one who acts on it), this man shall be blessed in his deed (in his doing)' — the true believer gazing intently into the gospel and abiding in it, the blessing being in the doing itself, so that the obedient doer, not the mere hearer, is blessed (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty, and continueth therein
not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work, this man shall be blessed in his deed
the blessed doer who looks into the perfect law of liberty
blessed in the doing itself

schools.summary: The searching test: 'if any man among you seem (thinks himself, or appears) to be religious (threskos — outwardly devout, observant of religious worship), and bridleth not (does not curb, rein in) his tongue, but deceiveth (deludes) his own heart, this man's religion (threskeia — religious observance) is vain (empty, worthless, futile)' — one may appear devout yet fail to control his speech, the unbridled tongue exposing the hollowness of mere outward religion; such a man deceiving himself, imagining his outward devotion is true religion while his ungoverned tongue betrays him; his religion, however outwardly observant, being worthless, for an uncontrolled tongue empties profession of all value (true religion governing the tongue) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: If any man among you seem to be religious, and bridleth not his tongue, but deceiveth his own heart
this man's religion is vain
unbridled speech makes religion vain
the ungoverned tongue exposing empty religion

schools.summary: The definition: 'pure (katara — clean) religion and undefiled (amiantos — unstained) before God and the Father (in God's sight, by his judgment) is this, To visit (episkeptesthai — care for, look after, relieve) the fatherless (orphans) and widows in their affliction (distress), and to keep himself unspotted (aspilon — unstained) from the world (its corruption, defilement, sinful ways)' — James defining the religion genuine and acceptable to God (not the whole of religion but its characteristic marks, over against vain religion, v.26): active compassion to the helpless and needy (practical love to those who cannot repay, the fruit of true religion in deeds of mercy) joined to personal purity (keeping oneself unstained from the world's pollution) — true religion uniting compassion to the needy with separation from sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: true worship before God is shown in mercy to the orphan and widow and in keeping oneself unspotted from the world — the religion God approves being not outward observance but active love to the helpless joined with purity of life, the inward devotion that bears fruit in compassion and holiness.

themes: Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this
To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep himself unspotted from the world
the description of pure religion: mercy and purity
compassion joined to personal holiness
